summary: I was born in Chicago, Illinois, specifically at St. Luke's Hospital on December 3, 1931. Later, I worked in the Chicago Public Schools for about half a year before being drafted into the Army in 1953. My wife also taught in Chicago Public Schools before joining me overseas.

Illinois is where I was born and where I spent part of my early life, including my time working in the Chicago Public Schools. When I was drafted in 1953, I left Illinois to begin my military service. My wife also returned to Illinois to live with her parents and work in the family flower shop before joining me overseas.

I was born at St. Luke's Hospital in Chicago on December 3, 1931. This hospital is where my life began before I eventually went on to serve in the Army. It holds significance as the place of my birth in Illinois.

I was drafted into the United States Army in 1953 while attending school and married for nine months. I went through basic training at Fort Leonard Wood and later trained as an aircraft mechanic at San Marcos Air Force Base in Texas. Eventually, I was stationed in Mannheim, Germany, working on Army aircraft, while my wife became an Army civilian employee teaching children of servicemen.

Before my husband was drafted, I taught for a year in Cleveland, Ohio. This was part of my early teaching career before moving on to teach in Chicago Public Schools. My teaching experience in Cleveland helped me secure a teaching position later in Germany.

I underwent my basic training at Fort Leonard Wood in 1953. My wife would travel there on weekends to stay with me, along with a friend whose husband was also in training. Basic training was tough but typical, focusing on survival and weapons training.

My wife and her friend traveled together to St. Louis, Missouri, to visit us during our basic training weekends at Fort Leonard Wood. St. Louis was a stopover point on their trips to see their husbands before returning to their jobs.

After basic training, I was sent to San Marcos Air Force Base in Texas to train as an aircraft mechanic, despite having no prior mechanical experience. This training prepared me for my later work repairing Army aircraft in Germany.

I spent some time in Texas while my husband trained at San Marcos Air Force Base. I worked as a florist in San Antonio and Austin, trying to find teaching jobs but was unsuccessful. Later, I returned to my parents' home in Illinois before deciding to travel to Germany to be with my husband.

While my husband was training in Texas, I worked in a floral shop in San Antonio. This was part of my effort to support myself while hoping to find a teaching position. It was during this time I realized I wanted to join my husband overseas.

Alongside San Antonio, I also worked in Austin, Texas, at a floral shop while my husband was at San Marcos Air Force Base. This was before I eventually left for Germany to be with him. My time in Austin was part of my varied efforts to find work.

I was stationed at Coleman Barracks in Mannheim, Germany, where I worked repairing Army aircraft. My wife joined me there after securing a teaching position through the United States Army Europe school system. We lived off base in a small town called Blumenau, near Mannheim.

Coleman Barracks in Mannheim was my base in Germany where I worked as an aircraft mechanic. It was an Army Air Force base where I repaired light aircraft like the L-19, L-17, and H-21 helicopters. My wife was also stationed nearby as an Army civilian teacher.

I traveled to Germany by ship from France, specifically on the Liberte Ship of the French line. The journey took seven days, and I arrived in Germany around the beginning of February after missing the initial boat from Chicago. This trip was my way of joining my husband overseas despite my parents' objections.

On the ship to Germany, I met Cal Lancaster, the superintendent of the USA Europe School. He was returning to Germany after attending his mother's funeral in the States. He offered me a teaching position if I could verify my credentials, which I did, and he enrolled me as an Army civilian teacher at Mannheim schools.

I worked as a Department of Army Civilian employee under United States Army Europe, teaching children of servicemen stationed in Germany. Cal Lancaster, the superintendent, managed the schools I taught at. My husband was also stationed in the same area, which allowed us to live together off base.

We lived in an apartment in Blumenau, a small town near Mannheim, Germany. This was a great privilege since I was able to live off base with my wife. The apartment was previously occupied by someone rotating back to the U.S., and it allowed us a more normal life during the assignment.

During my time in Germany, there were alerts when the Russian Army was besieging the eastern part of Germany. In those situations, troops would report to their positions and prepare to move, but my aircraft group usually stayed back to service planes. These alerts were brief and felt almost like a vacation compared to normal Army life.

Part of my job in Germany was repairing the L-19 Bird Dog aircraft, among other light Army aircraft. I learned to rebuild engines and maintain these planes despite having no prior mechanical experience before the Army. This training and work gave me valuable technical skills.

I was responsible for repairing the L-17 Navion aircraft while stationed at Coleman Barracks in Germany. This was part of my broader role maintaining various Army light aircraft. The work required precision and adherence to Army procedures.

Among the aircraft I repaired was the Piasecki H-21 helicopter. My duties included engine rebuilding and ensuring these helicopters were flight-ready. This was a significant responsibility, especially given my initial lack of mechanical experience before joining the Army.
